data.title: Hershey CEO on Making Luxury Chocolate Accessible — and Battling Ozempic Breath — WSJ
data.description:
By Jesse Newman

Trips to mouth. Democratizing luxury. Ozempic breath. It sounds like refrigerator magnet poetry, but these are a few of the things on Hershey Chief Executive Kirk Tanner's mind.

The chocolate giant is navigating GLP-1 weight-loss drugs, deflated consumer sentiment, and a roller-coaster cocoa market. Tanner is trying to divine what consumers want, while federal food policy shifts and a member of the Reese's family sounds off about the company's famed peanut butter candy.

Tanner inherited the job less than a year ago, joining Hershey from Wendy's. Before that, he spent more than three decades working in soda, snacks and food service at PepsiCo.

Hershey is leaning heavily on insights from its commercial intelligence team, a group that closely tracks forces from GLP-1 drugs to new restrictions on food-stamp purchases. The team is conducting video interviews with consumers, and Tanner himself is watching.

The Wall Street Journal talked to Tanner about premium chocolate, dealmaking and how the company can help tackle "Ozempic breath" — a not-so-savory side effect some people experience with weight loss medications.

WSJ: You're tracking GLP-1 drugs closely. What are you learning?

KIRK TANNER: We've started to see some traction around our refreshment brand, Ice Breakers. Gum and mints, our business is doing very well. This will be a billion-dollar brand.

The other part of our portfolio that's really working right now is popcorn, LesserEvil and SkinnyPop, because you can have a lot of snacking — "trips to mouth" is what we do call that. Your brain is telling you, hey, I can have popcorn because I can have a lot of little occasions without it filling me up. And then functional snacking, like proteins, are really important.

WSJ: How are consumers responding to new restrictions on what shoppers can buy with food stamps, including sweets in some states?

TANNER: We're interviewing SNAP consumers. It's important to think about the choices that consumers make, and I think that there's some choiceful spending. It's definitely not an exit of the category. Consumers are adjusting their pack sizes and channels, but they're still celebrating the occasions.

WSJ: Talk about your focus on premium products, at a time when more consumers are affordability-minded. And any hints on the top-secret premium Hershey bar coming this fall?

TANNER: Premium is not just for highly affluent people shopping in unique channels. The Hershey brand is approachable. Consumers give it the license to elevate and it's going to be appropriately priced, so it's not going to be out of reach. That is what I think makes sense for consumers. They want to have those premium experiences without having to spend a fortune.

These [new] products are cream-filled. Now, are these bars going to be outrageously priced? No, they're going to be accessible premium.

WSJ: A member of the Reese's family has targeted Hershey, complaining that the company has watered down the recipe for some Reese's products. How are you dealing with that?

KIRK TANNER: I stay focused on what consumers are saying. Always. That's the game. Not what someone's saying, not what someone's trying to get attention on. That's why we're interviewing consumers. That's why we're looking at many multiple sources of data that triangulate exactly how consumers are feeling, how they're voting with their dollars, where the growth is. Our two biggest brands are growing double digits — Reese's and Hershey.

WSJ: Dealmaking has picked up in the food industry. Is Hershey open to a deal with Mondelez?

KIRK TANNER: Our focus is right now on our current portfolio. We feel like we can deliver our outlook without a big event. We've got a terrific portfolio that we're building. We're in a good place. We've had a terrific first quarter. We have a really good '26 plan. We feel really good about the next few years. So we're not looking for something that will save the day. No.

Write to Jesse Newman at jesse.newman@wsj.com

data.title: GSK plc reports results for the quarter ended March 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• GSK plc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 68 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended March 31, lower than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of $1.13. The mean expectation of two analysts for the quarter was for earnings of $1.16 per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 65 cents to 69 cents per share.

  • Revenue rose 10.3% to $11.46 billion from a year ago; analysts expected $11.52 billion.

  • GSK plc's reported EPS for the quarter was 42 cents​.

  • GSK plc shares had fallen by 6.9% this quarter and gained 4.8% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 85.6% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no earnings estimate revisions by analysts covering the company.

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 5 "strong buy" or "buy," 4 "hold" and 1 "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the pharmaceuticals peer group is also "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for GSK plc is $52.45, about 2% above its last closing price of $51.40

data.title: AUTOMATIC DATA PROCESSING INC 1Q 2026: Revenue $5.94B, EPS $3.38— 10-Q Summary
data.description:
Automatic Data Processing Inc reported first-quarter 2026 results with revenue of $5.94B and diluted EPS of $3.38, each up versus the year‑ago quarter as the company cited sustained demand, pricing gains and investments in AI and service capabilities.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenue: $5.94B for quarter ended Mar 31, 2026, up from $5.55B in year‑ago quarter (7% YoY).
  • Net income: $1.36B for quarter ended Mar 31, 2026, versus $1.25B in year‑ago quarter (9% YoY).
  • Diluted EPS: $3.38 for quarter ended Mar 31, 2026, up from $3.06 in year‑ago quarter (10% YoY).

Business Highlights

  • Revenue growth was 7% year‑to‑date (6% organic constant currency), driven by new bookings and strong client retention.
  • Employer Services and PEO growth benefited from pricing, pays‑per‑control gains and higher interest on client funds.
  • The company expanded AI and technology offerings, launching ADP Assist, broadening ADP Lyric HCM and an AI marketplace, and scaling GenAI across service operations to improve payroll and HR efficiency.
  • ADP increased investments in service, implementation and sales to support growth; R&D spending rose to develop next‑generation products and integrate WorkForce Software.

Original SEC Filing: AUTOMATIC DATA PROCESSING INC [ ADP ] - 10-Q - Apr. 30, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Chicago grain futures fall on oil retreat, improved US weather outlook
data.description:
Key points:
  • Soy complex choppy as oil prices turn lower
  • Improved US weather forecasts ease planting concerns
  • Funds have been net sellers of corn, wheat and soymeal

By P.J. Huffstutter

Chicago Board of Trade grain futures reversed course on Thursday following a retreat in crude oil prices amid a flurry of technical trading and forecasts of improving weather across the central U.S., market analysts said.

The CBOT's most-active wheat contract settled 16-1/4 cents lower at $6.36-3/4 per bushel after hitting its highest price since June 2024 the previous session.

Pressure from the wheat market spilled over to corn futures, where the most-active contract ended down 3 cents at $4.74-3/4 a bushel, after rallying to a near one-year high on Wednesday.

Meanwhile, trading in soybean futures was choppy, with many contracts trading either side of unchanged early in the session.

The most-active soybean contract on the Chicago Board of Trade settled down 1-1/2 cents at $11.95-1/2 a bushel after hitting its highest point in more than six weeks.

Global oil prices jumped to a four-year high of more than $126 a barrel on Thursday on concerns that the Iran war could worsen and lead to a protracted Gulf supply disruption that could hurt global economic growth, but later retreated.

The strength of oil prices, which have risen on supply disruption linked to the Iran war, has buoyed the soy complex and corn futures, as soybeans and corn are feedstocks for biofuel.

Early planting of U.S. soybeans and corn has progressed well, and forecasts for drier weather for the U.S. Corn Belt through this weekend eased some market concerns that a spate of storms in the Midwest could delay seeding in some areas, market analysts said.

"To see some level of consolidation is not surprising," said Angie Setzer, partner at advisory firm Consus Ag Consulting. "Cooler heads are prevailing with the weather, too."

Export demand also remained relatively steady. The U.S. Department of Agriculture reported net export sales of old-crop U.S. wheat in the week ended April 23 at 226,100 metric tons and new-crop sales of 156,700 tons, both in line with trade expectations.

The USDA also reported net export sales of old-crop U.S. corn in the week ended April 23 at 1,597,800 tons, in line with expectations for 1,000,000 to 1,900,000 tons.

And USDA reported net export sales of old-crop U.S. soybeans in the week ended April 23 at 258,100 metric tons, toward the low end of trade expectations for 200,000 to 600,000 tons.

Commodity funds were net sellers of corn, wheat and soymeal, traders said on Wednesday. (CBOT/FUNDS)

data.title: US Senate bans its members, staff from betting in prediction markets
data.description:
Key points:
  • Move follows fraud case over soldier who allegedly placed bet on operation in Venezuela
  • Senate urges House, executive, and judicial branches to adopt similar bans
  • Bills that would ban or curtail House members from trading stocks remain stalled

By Richard Cowan

The U.S. Senate on Thursday unanimously approved an immediate prohibition on incumbent senators, staff and other officers from using prediction markets that provide a form of gambling on real-world events.

The change in Senate rules to impose the senators' ban was spearheaded by Republican Senator Bernie Moreno of Ohio, and an amendment by Democratic Senator Alex Padilla broadening the ban to Senate staff was included in the measure.

Senators also urged the House of Representatives and the executive and judicial branches of government to do the same.

“United States senators have no business engaging in speculative activities like prediction markets while collecting a taxpayer-funded paycheck," Moreno said in a Senate speech in which he called for an immediate vote on the rules change.

Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer endorsed the effort, saying, "We must never allow Congress to turn into a casino where members representing the public can gamble on wars or economic crises."

On Tuesday, a U.S. Army soldier pleaded not guilty to fraud charges stemming from his $400,000 in wins after allegedly using confidential information late last year for a bet that Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro would soon be out of office.

The activity occurred just before the U.S. military operation on January 3 that resulted in his removal.

For years, Congress has been debating whether to impose a ban on lawmakers' stock trades, which proponents characterize as insider trading. Members of Congress get closed briefings on possible military matters, for example, and know in advance the timing of legislation impacting companies might advance toward enactment.

A bipartisan stock trading ban bill had been moving ahead in the House late last year. It fell apart, however, when a committee that oversees such efforts instead advanced a partisan Republican measure that has not yet been put to a vote in the full House.

In 2012, Congress passed legislation imposing new disclosures on stock trades, which sometimes are ignored by lawmakers or filed long after deadlines.

Backers of a full ban on lawmaker stock trades estimate that 113 members of Congress made 9,261 trades involving 706 million shares during 2024 alone.

In April 2025, President Donald Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform that "This is a great time to buy!!!" hours before he announced a pause on tariffs he had imposed. The posting raised concerns that it prompted a high volume of stock market trades.

data.title: Ackman blames retail traders for fund's weak Wall Street debut
data.description:
Key points:
  • Ackman details trading patterns after weak debut
  • Newly listed Pershing Square USA rebounds on second day
  • Ackman and his employees put $500 million into IPO

By Nell Mackenzie

Billionaire Bill Ackman on Thursday blamed retail investors for the roughly 18% drop in his newly listed closed-end fund's stock price a day earlier, adding that he expects shares to rebound.

"Retail investors don't know how to invest in IPOs," he said on a call with foreign journalists one day after listing the new stock-picking fund, as well as his management company, on the New York Stock Exchange.

Ackman marketed his U.S. stock market listing as a chance for retail investors to access investments normally limited to the ultrawealthy - even though his fund has underperformed the broad stock market in the past several years.

Over the last eight years, Ackman said his closed-end fund has returned an average of 25% a year, beating other funds' performance.

The investor said he thought retail investors overcommitted to his IPO, got the shares they said they wanted but then didn't have the cash to pay for them and were therefore forced to sell.

The new fund, Pershing Square USA , was priced at $50 but shares sank to $40.90 on Wednesday. They traded up more than 6% on Thursday at $43.54, still below the IPO price.

"We had a whole bunch of people dump that stock yesterday for technical reasons," he said.

He also said institutional investors made up more than 80% of the capital, raising some questions about how retail investors could be blamed for Wednesday's sharp drop.

Investors who bought five shares of Pershing Square USA at the initial public offering were gifted one share of his management company Pershing Square Inc (PS.N). That stock rose 20% to $29.18 after opening trading at $24 on Wednesday.

The $5 billion raised in the IPO swelled Ackman's overall assets by 25%.

Ackman on Thursday flew to Canada and held his conference call with foreign journalists on the tarmac in Montreal in order to comply with U.S. securities regulations. No U.S. journalists were permitted to listen.

On the call, he said he and his employees committed roughly $500 million in cash to Pershing Square USA.

On Thursday he said fellow fund manager Marc Lasry and tech mogul Mark Zuckerberg's family office ICONIQ Capital invested in the stock.

The new fund's investments will closely mirror what Ackman already holds in another fund, Pershing Square Holdings , a closed-end fund listed in London.

The main holdings include Google parent Alphabet and Zuckerberg's Meta Platforms .

Pershing Square Holdings rose an annualized 7% from 2015 through 2025, underperforming the S&P 500's 11% increase over that time. Including dividends, the S&P 500's lead would be more. Strong returns from bets on AI have not been enough to offset losing bets Ackman made a decade ago related to companies such as Valeant Pharmaceuticals and Herbalife .

Looking ahead, Ackman said he is likely to launch another fund within a year, possibly even before the end of the year.

data.title: Brazil's Suzano predicts solid pulp demand after Q1 miss sinks shares
data.description:
Brazilian pulp maker Suzano expects solid demand for its main product in the coming months, the firm's chief executive said on Thursday, even as Suzano posted a 6% decline in its first-quarter core profit that missed market forecasts.

CEO Joao Alberto de Abreu told a press conference that Suzano, the world's largest pulp producer, plans to raise prices by different amounts across its regions, citing geopolitical tensions in its markets.

PULP DEMAND REMAINS STRONG

  • Abreu said Europe and the United States are showing stronger demand, likely due to merchandise flow changes and restocking movements.

  • In April , Suzano hiked pulp prices in Europe and North America by $50 per metric ton, but opted for a lower $20 increase in Asia.

  • Abreu said the company was being more cautious about price hikes in Asia after successful hiking campaigns in Europe and North America.

INVESTORS BEARISH ON Q1 RESULTS

  • Investors reacted negatively on Thursday to Suzano's earnings report, as the firm reported a weaker net revenue in the quarter despite higher pulp sales volume.

  • Earnings ere hit by a stronger Brazilian currency that diluted its export revenues.

  • Suzano's Sao Paulo-listed shares fell up to 4%, making it the biggest loser in the benchmark stock index Bovespa , which was up 1.5%.

  • JPMorgan's analysts said in a note they expect the "challenging quarter" to cause downward changes in consensus estimates.

data.title: CBOT wheat futures fall on profit-taking, forecasts for rainfall
data.description:
Chicago Board of Trade wheat futures turned lower on Thursday on a spate of profit-taking and technical trading, after they hit their highest point in nearly two years on a continuous chart during the previous session, traders said.

  • But the market continues to be underpinned by worries about drought threatening yield prospects in the southern U.S. Plains wheat belt.

  • Forecasts for improving weather and rainfall in the U.S. Plains wheat belt could alleviate some of the drought stress on crops, but some areas may have already experienced yield loss, market analysts said.

  • CBOT July wheat (WN26) settled 16-1/4 cents lower at $6.36-3/4 per bushel.

  • K.C. July wheat (KWN26) ended down 11-1/4 cents at $6.93-1/2 per bushel.

  • Minneapolis July spring wheat (MWEN26) settled 9-3/4 cents lower at $7.05-3/4 per bushel.

  • The U.S. Department of Agriculture reported net export sales of old-crop U.S. wheat in the week ended April 23 at 226,100 metric tons and new-crop sales of 156,700 tons, both in line with trade expectations.

  • The European Commission on Thursday cut its export forecasts for common wheat, or soft wheat, from the European Union for this season and next, with exports in 2025/26 now seen at 27 million metric tons this season, from 28 million tons projected last month. For 2026/27, the Commission pegged soft wheat exports at 29.65 million tons, a touch below the 30 million tons forecast in its initial outlook a month ago.

data.title: Microsoft slips despite strong earnings and outlook
data.description:
Microsoft is slipping a bit after earnings, even though, on paper, it actually delivered a strong quarter and a solid outlook.

The company reported EPS of $4.27 on revenue of $82.89 billion, up 18% Y/Y, both ahead of expectations. Cloud is still doing most of the heavy lifting. The Intelligent Cloud segment brought in $34.7 billion, with Azure growing 40% Y/Y, and Microsoft is guiding for that kind of growth to continue into the next quarter. Revenue guidance for the upcoming period also came in strong, at $86.7 billion to $87.8 billion.

So why the lukewarm reaction? It looks like the market is focusing more on what it's costing to get that growth. Cloud margins are expected around 64%, with pressure coming from heavier AI investments and rising usage of tools like GitHub Copilot. In simple terms, the growth story is intact, but it's getting more expensive to sustain.

Wall Street doesn't seem too worried yet. Analysts are still largely positive, pointing to strong demand that continues to outpace supply and a growing AI revenue stream that's starting to scale in a big way.

data.title: Eli Lilly Rises on FDA Compounding Proposal
data.description:
Eli Lilly (LLY, Financials) shares rose after the U.S. Food and Drug Administration proposed new restrictions that could limit pharmacy-made copies of popular GLP-1 drugs.

The FDA is considering removing tirzepatide, semaglutide and liraglutide from the 503B Bulk Drug Substances List. Drugs on that list can be compounded from bulk ingredients by certain pharmacies.

Tirzepatide is sold by Lilly as Zepbound for obesity and Mounjaro for type 2 diabetes. Semaglutide is sold by Novo Nordisk as Wegovy and Ozempic, while liraglutide is marketed as Saxenda and Victoza.

The FDA said there is no clinical need for the three drugs to remain on the list. The agency also noted that compounding is generally limited when drugs are not in shortage.

The proposal could reduce competition from lower-cost compounded versions and support demand for branded GLP-1 treatments. Investors will watch the FDA process and any impact on Lilly's obesity drug sales.

data.title: House Votes to Reopen Most of DHS, Ending Long Shutdown — WSJ
data.description:
By Terell Wright

WASHINGTON — The House approved a long-delayed measure to fund most of the Department of Homeland Security and end a 76-day shutdown, after the Trump administration warned it had run out of emergency funds to pay workers next week.

The bipartisan measure, which passed on a voice vote, funds operations including the Coast Guard, Federal Emergency Management Agency and Transportation Security Administration but not DHS's immigration-enforcement arms, as part of a two-track compromise backed by President Trump.

The Senate approved the legislation earlier this month but Speaker Mike Johnson (R., La.) had declined so far to take it up until Thursday, waiting for progress on a second track that would allow Republicans to fund Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol without Democratic votes.

The breakthrough came after the House took a major step late Wednesday toward funding immigration enforcement by passing a budget framework need to proceed to the actual legislation in May.

"We threw a fit and we had to," said Johnson about why he held up the broader DHS bill. "We passed the [budget] resolution first. That was critically important for us to do to ensure we will protect the homeland."

By passing the DHS bill on a voice vote, party leaders avoided forcing a tough vote for some members, who had seethed over the DHS funding being split into two chunks.

The bill now goes to President Trump's desk for his signature, and he is expected to sign it later Thursday. He had endorsed the two-step plan a month ago, effectively pressuring Johnson to accept the approach favored by Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R., S.D.).

DHS funding lapsed on Feb. 14 due to Democrats' opposition to funding ICE operations without changes to enforcement practices and oversight, following the deaths of two American citizens in Minnesota. In a memo earlier this week, the White House Office of Management and Budget said it "is imperative that Congress immediately fund DHS and its critical operations."

Pay lapses earlier this year at TSA had led to absenteeism and long lines at airport checkpoints. More than 1,000 officers quit since the shutdown began.

The next step for Republicans will be a budget-reconciliation bill designed to fund ICE and Border Patrol through the end of the Trump administration. Using that procedure allows Republicans to pass the measure with just a simple majority in the Senate, where legislation typically requires 60 votes, and President Trump has said he wants the measure on his desk by June 1. The same technique was used by the GOP to pass Trump's tax law last year.

Republicans' sharp divisions on issues ranging from crop pesticides to privacy concerns slowed GOP efforts this week to meet key legislative deadlines on not only DHS but also an overseas spying program and the farm bill. But with passage of those measures, Johnson put an upbeat spin on the proceedings.

"In spite of our razor-thin, historically small majority, House Republicans continue to deliver for the American people," Johnson said on Thursday. "The equations we solved on legislation this week were virtually impossible," he said.

Democrats said House Republicans were just doing what they should have done weeks earlier.

"The House GOP finally caved in to reality and joined House Democrats, Senate Democrats and Senate Republicans in voting to fund the rest of DHS except for the dangerously lawless ICE and Border Patrol," said Rep. Jamie Raskin (D., Md.).

Write to Terell Wright at terell.wright@wsj.com

data.title: 'From Life Itself' Review: A Neighborhood in Erdogan's Turkey
data.description:
By Tunku Varadarajan

Among the many contentious strongmen who have popped up worldwide in the first decades of the 21st century, Recep Tayyip Erdogan of Turkey is the most confounding. He presides over a land that once forged a massive empire but which today is reduced to the status of a thwarted power. His illiberal Turkey is an in-between place that straddles the fading promise of the West and the backwardness of the Muslim world.

Ottoman imperial greatness, long gone, has been replaced by what Suzy Hansen calls the "personal ideology of Islamic sentimentality and post-Western revenge" that characterizes Mr. Erdogan. His political mode is "fantastical" autocracy and a rejection of liberal democracy.

Ms. Hansen, an American writer and reporter, is the author of "From Life Itself," an exhaustive, passionate study of the Istanbul neighborhood of Karagümrük, which she regards as a microcosm of Turkey in the "age of Erdogan." Karagümrük isn't a part of Istanbul that tourists ever visit: It is, Ms. Hansen tells us, "mixed up and broken down," known for "mafias, right-wing nationalism, and thieves"; a place where "all eyes face outside, to the street, where life happens." The rest of Istanbul is "arriviste" compared with Karagümrük, older than almost anywhere else in the city.

It is also a neighborhood with its own arrivistes — or newcomers. Like other parts of Istanbul, Karagümrük has received waves of coarse, rural migrants over the years, from Anatolia and the Black Sea coast, drawn to Istanbul for a better life. In the eyes of the established residents of the city, as quoted by Ms. Hansen, these migrants lower the tone with their manners and dress, and raise the levels of piety and conservatism. The latest arrivals, in their tens of thousands, have been refugees from Syria, escapees from a war (now concluded) between a violent dictator and his equally bloodthirsty Islamist opponents.

The Syrian presence in Karagümrük creates real tensions — cultural, linguistic, religious. The author suggests, also, that tensions are "racial" — a progressive American impulse. But the real social division in Istanbul isn't nationalist; it's the age-old chasm that separates the original, multigenerational city-dwellers from the new arrivals from the countryside (of which Mr. Erdogan was one, born to parents who moved to Istanbul from the northeastern coastal town of Rize, near Georgia). In a hilarious illustration of this disconnect, Ms. Hansen writes of a yokel who poured "twenty bags of soil" on the balcony of his Karagümrük apartment to plant beans, tomatoes and peppers. Inevitably, the soil rained down on the heads of his neighbors below.

Ms. Hansen's title is a fragment of a 1937 speech by Mustafa Kemal Atatürk, the founder of modern Turkey, in which he declared that his project to Europeanize the Turks derived its inspiration "not from the heavens . . . but directly from life itself." His forced Westernization of a Muslim civitas had been audacious and ruthless; it erased the old ways of thinking and praying, as well as Turkey's forms of dress, and replaced its old Perso-Arabic script with a Latin-based alphabet.

If the loss of empire was, as Ms. Hansen writes, "devastating" to Turks, the widespread ripping out of a centuries-old culture was arguably more traumatic, especially as it became a political taboo to mourn that loss. As Ms. Hansen describes in her book, Turks have since lived with an unresolved angst, an existential tension between Islam and a secularism that has tended to mimic the harsh laïcité of France — a banishment of religion from the public sphere.

The name "Atatürk," bestowed by Turkey's Grand National Assembly in 1934, means "Father of the Turks," and it is Ms. Hansen's argument that Mr. Erdogan is committing ideological patricide. There is, she writes, "something profound" happening under Mr. Erdogan. He is killing off Atatürk by undoing Kemalism, Turkey's dominant, secularist ideology. As she puts it, Mr. Erdogan is "fracturing and reinventing Turkey's seemingly unassailable national identity."

Like many Western liberals — and she is an unapologetic exemplar — Ms. Hansen has found herself torn by Mr. Erdogan from time to time. After all, Kemalism, for all its hostility to archaic belief, was an elitist set of values imposed on a population that had no say in the matter. What Atatürk dismissed as the "religion of the Arabs" had millions of adherents in Turkey, particularly in the agrarian hinterland. To Western observers such as Ms. Hansen, Mr. Erdogan's political emergence — first as mayor of Istanbul in 1994 — seemed like a democratic corrective, a triumph of public opinion (however Islamist) over undemocratic Kemalism. She (and others) briefly believed that "Turkey would emerge as a Middle East success story, a template for Western fantasies of a liberal Muslim nation."

As the power of the Turkish military began to recede, Ms. Hansen — new to the country and "hanging out with leftists who blamed the military" for all of Turkey's ills — came to think that Mr. Erdogan "seemed decidedly on the right side of history." She acknowledges that she was hornswoggled, writing that "Erdogan's democracy was democracy for a day" — by which she means that he won power by the ballot box only to subvert democracy once in power.

By 2014, she says, "the abstemious, incorruptible leader had become a louche, boorish one." A 2016 coup attempt led to the imprisonment of thousands of political dissidents as "terrorists." Ms. Hansen's account of Mr. Erdogan's purge of the universities is harrowing.

If Mr. Erdogan is the diabolical figure who looms over Ms. Hansen's narrative, the heart of her book lies in the characters who people her account of Karagümrük, each one standing for a strand of Turkish political reality. The most endearing among them is Ismail Altintoprak, the muhtar, or elected headman, of Karagümrük. An elderly man who sits "with his cane and his Marlboro Reds," he embodies an Ottoman-esque courtliness at odds with the intolerant and corrupt vulgarity of the Erdogan era. Politically independent, he is a descendant of the Crimean Tatars who once dominated the neighborhood alongside Albanians and Bosnians and others from "the outskirts of the Ottoman Empire." His vision of the world, writes Ms. Hansen, draws on "the time of cosmopolitanism, not nationalism."

Alongside him, by way of contrast, is Hüseyin, the owner of a supermarket and an Erdogan enthusiast. One of the few Turks in Karagümrük who socializes with the Syrians, he argues that the latter — devout Sunni Muslims who flock to the local mosques — are "our votes in the future." Hüseyin understands instinctively that the Syrians are an electoral bank for the Islamist Mr. Erdogan, and grasps also that the president is bulking up his own support base within Turkey by admitting into the country three million refugees who would never vote for a secular party.

Other characters include the estimable Ebru, a Karagümrük realtor who is the most educated woman in the neighborhood. She challenges Mr. Altintoprak in the election for muhtar but loses with grace. Hers is a voice of secular sanity. She is kind toward the Syrians while fully aware of the problems they bring with them, not least the political advantage they confer on Mr. Erdogan. Like the rest of secular Turkey, Ebru is sad and deflated. "The Turkish nation created in 1923 never healed the wounds of a people who lost an empire," writes Ms. Hansen. Ebru is one of those people, as is Mr. Altintoprak. They live in a Turkey that isn't their country anymore. It belongs to Mr. Erdogan.

  • Mr. Varadarajan, a Journal contributor, is a fellow at the American Enterprise Institute and at NYU Law School's Classical Liberal Institute.

data.title: Stocks are mixed as global tensions weigh on sentiment
data.description:
Markets are looking a bit uneasy Thursday, with geopolitical tensions back in focus and oil pushing higher, and that backdrop is showing up clearly in how stocks are moving.

On the upside, a few names are doing the heavy lifting. Hertz is jumping about 18% after expanding its partnership with Uber, leaning into both driver-led fleets and robotaxi operations. Qualcomm is up roughly 14% after a solid earnings beat, with strength in automotive and IoT helping offset weaker handset demand. Eli Lilly is also having a strong day, up about 9% after another big quarter driven by its GLP-1 drugs, while Altria is gaining around 7% on steady pricing and earnings growth. Even Alphabet is moving higher, up about 5% after delivering a clean earnings beat with strong cloud momentum.

On the flip side, the pressure is just as clear. Check Point is down around 17% after a mixed quarter, while Meta is sliding about 10% as investors react more to rising spending than strong results. Teladoc is also under pressure, off about 6% after a softer outlook.
